summary: This lesson introduces the 'would have' construction (habría + past participle) and uses it to build complex structures: would have gone, would have been able to, would have liked. It also derives 'should' (debería) from deber + conditional, practicing it extensively.

teachings.summary: Haber contracts in the conditional just like poder: habería -> habría. This gives 'would have', which combines with past participles (-ado/-ido) to express 'would have done'. Habría serves I/he/she/it/you(formal). Other forms: habríamos (we), habrían (they), habrías (you informal).
teachings.rule: Habría + past participle = would have done. Habría ido = would have gone. Habríamos comido = would have eaten. Build complex sentences block by block.

examples.english: We would have been able to go.
They would have been able to eat.
They would have come to eat.
I would have liked it. (It would have pleased me.)
We would have liked them. (They would have pleased us.)

teachings.notes: With gustar in conditional perfect, think in Spanglish: 'I would have liked them' = 'They would have pleased us' = 'Nos habrían gustado'. Always start by identifying who is doing the verb.

teachings.summary: 'Should' is expressed as 'would owe': deber (must/owe) in the conditional = debería. Unlike English where 'should' drops 'to' (I should eat, not I should to eat), Spanish keeps the infinitive after debería.
teachings.rule: Debería = I/he/she should. Deberías = you should. Deberíamos = we should. Deberían = they should. Always followed by an infinitive (deberíamos hablar, not deberíamos hablado).

examples.english: You should come to my house.

examples.spanish: Deberías venir a mi casa a comer.
examples.english: You should come to my house to eat.

teachings.notes: In English, after can/should/must you don't say 'to' (I should eat). In Spanish, you still use the infinitive (deberíamos hablar). Using 'con él/con ella' instead of 'le' removes the ambiguity of le (him/her/you formal).

teachings.summary: Haber loses its -e- in the conditional, just like poder: habería -> habría. This is the same contraction pattern.
teachings.rule: Haber -> habr- + conditional endings. Habría, habrías, habríamos, habrían.

vocabulary.english: they would give it to me
